concerns.analysis:
The source document contains several meaningful flowchart diagrams on pages 10 through 16 that illustrate the water flow network and major nodes. In the rendered HTML, these images are entirely missing and have been replaced by text placeholders (e.g., `

[Image: ...]

`) instead of valid `` elements. This results in the loss of critical visual information.
concerns.source_pdf_quote: Flowchart diagram titled "Flow Chart of Major Nodes in the Upper Cape Fear Basin"
concerns.output_quote: [Image: A flow chart diagram showing the water flow network of major nodes in the Upper Cape Fear Basin...]

concerns.description: Output has more images than source PDF (ratio 4.5x) — possible image duplication
concerns.analysis: image_count_ratio=4.50. More images in output than in source indicates duplicated or fabricated image elements.

Checked for lost visual emphasis, particularly underlines and demoted headings. Several headings in the source PDF are formatted with an underline to emphasize the section transitions (e.g., 'Static and Pattern Data', 'Timeseries Data', 'Simulation:'). The extraction does capture them as headings but completely loses the underline formatting, which is a genuine structural emphasis loss since HTML/Markdown has specific formatting for underlines (`<u>`) that is omitted here.
concerns.source_pdf_quote: The phrases "Static and Pattern Data" (Page 7), "Timeseries Data" (Page 8), "Operating Rules" (Page 8), "Simulation:" (Page 17), and "Position Analysis:" (Page 17) are underlined in the source.
concerns.output_quote: The text fields simply read "Static and Pattern Data", "Timeseries Data", "Operating Rules", "Simulation:", and "Position Analysis:" with no mechanism to preserve the underline formatting.

concerns.analysis: header_footer_count=18. Header/footer elements are present in extraction data. This is common in multi-page government PDFs and is typically cosmetic — the renderer strips them from HTML output.
